title: JTBD Analysis: Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (NAICS 531120)

sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape
sections.content:
### 1.1 CFJ, Output Types, Offering Level

| NAICS | CFJ (extracted from description) | Output Types | Offering Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 531120 | Provide leasable nonresidential building space to tenant organizations while maintaining regulatory-compliant safety infrastructure and preserving long-term asset value | Class A Office, Class B/C Office, Retail Commercial, Industrial Flex, Medical Office | L7 (Ecosystem) — lessor coordinates owners, tenants, FM providers, regulators; does not produce a physical product |

Routing: B2B operator with departments → proceed through all Phase 1 substeps. Skip 1.4–1.5 (service NAICS, no Product BOM).

---

### 1.2 Segments

Defined by circumstance (operational context + FM model), not portfolio size or geography.

| # | Segment | Defining Circumstance | Türwächter Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1 | Institutional / REIT multi-building portfolio | Dozens–hundreds of properties; centralized FM team; compliance tracked at portfolio level | HIGH — portfolio dashboard and centralized compliance reporting are the primary value lever |
| S2 | Single-asset / small-portfolio private lessor | 1–5 buildings; FM outsourced; decisions made per-building | MEDIUM — value argument more price-driven; IoT premium harder to justify |
| S3 | Medical office park lessor | Patient-access corridors; HIPAA overlay; fire marshal audits more frequent | HIGH — regulatory pressure intensity is highest; battery-powered wireless is preferred over mains for retrofit |
| S4 | Mixed-use retail / commercial lessor | High pedestrian throughput; guest-facing stairwell exits; frequent misuse | HIGH — frequent alarm events; detection lag most painful |
| S5 | Class A smart-office campus lessor | BMS-native tenants; KNX/BACnet infrastructure; tenants demand IoT-integrated systems | HIGH (with friction) — IoT value clear but EasyWave protocol lock-in (C6) creates BMS integration barrier |
| S6 | Industrial flex / logistics park lessor | High daily exit-door traffic; infrequent maintenance visits; steel-frame buildings | MEDIUM — battery life (C4) and RF attenuation (C3) elevate constraint exposure |

---

### 1.3 Process VN

Reference: `VN_lessors_nonresidential_buildings.md` (completed). Key structure:

```
L7  Commercial Property Leasing Ecosystem
├── L6a  Property Acquisition
├── L6b  Asset Preparation
├── L6c  Tenant Acquisition
├── L6d  Building Operations   ← Emergency Exit Security sits here
│        └── L5  Emergency Exit Security
│                 ├── L4  Door Guardian Alarm Unit   ← Türwächter anchors here
│                 ├── L4  Wireless Monitoring Receiver
│                 ├── L4  BUS Gateway & Aggregator
│                 └── L4  Signal Repeater
├── L6e  Tenant Services
├── L6H1 Regulatory & Compliance (H)
└── L6H2 Financial Management (H)
```

Steps 1.4 (Product BOM) and 1.5 (Edges): **SKIPPED** — service NAICS (531120 outputs are occupancy outcomes, not physical products).

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | PROCESS_VN (product serves Building Operations; never becomes part of the tenant's space) |
| **Anchor level** | L4 |
| **Anchor node** | Door Guardian Alarm Unit |
| **Parent L5** | Emergency Exit Security |
| **Parent L6** | Building Operations (L6d) |
| **Anchor rationale** | Türwächter is a component (L4) within the Emergency Exit Security system (L5). It requires Wireless Monitoring Receiver + BUS Gateway to form the complete L5 solution. Does not anchor at L5 — it is not the full system. |
| **Job context** | Parent unit (Emergency Exit Security, L5) defines the job: everything the product must do derives from what Emergency Exit Security needs within L6d Building Operations. |

---

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Function verb** | Secure (from Emergency Exit Security L5 job_family: "secure emergency exits against unauthorized use while maintaining unimpeded evacuation capability") |
| **Beneficiary** | Building Operations team — specifically the Facility Manager |
| **Lens** | "What securing does a Building Operations team need to achieve for emergency exits across a multi-tenant commercial property portfolio?" |
| **Segment filter — Level A** | Building Operations (L6d) activates for ALL output types — every building type has mandated emergency exits |
| **Segment filter — Level B** | Wireless IoT variant best fits: S1 (portfolio), S3 (medical office), S5 (smart-office with BMS integration budget). Less suited: S2 (price-sensitive), S6 (RF constraints). |

---

### 2.3 Core Job + Product Job

**Product Job** (anchored at L4, parent = Emergency Exit Security L5):

> *"Help Emergency Exit Security to prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors across a multi-tenant commercial property while maintaining unimpeded evacuation compliance, providing portfolio-level status visibility, and generating audit-ready compliance records — without requiring mains wiring at each door point"*

**Abstraction test (passes):** 5 alternatives from different categories identified below. If only one category of alternative existed, it would be a scenario, not a Core Job.

---

### 2.4 Alternatives (5 per Core Job)

| # | Alternative | Product Group | Inherent Trade-offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Mechanical door alarm guards, no connectivity (~40% share) | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards | No remote monitoring; compliance issues discovered during physical inspection (hours–days lag); no battery monitoring; lowest per-door cost |
| A2 | **Wireless IoT exit alarm guards ← Türwächter** (~20% share) | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards | Battery dependency; EasyWave protocol not natively interoperable with BACnet/KNX (C6); 30 m RF range limit (C3); higher per-door cost (~730 EUR, C7) |
| A3 | Wired electronic exit control, alarm-panel-integrated (~25% share) | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards | Requires mains wiring + conduit to each door; electrician for install and maintenance; not retrofit-friendly in occupied buildings; full FACP integration possible |
| A4 | Integrated alarmed panic hardware (e-Bar type) (~15% share) | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards | Replaces entire panic bar assembly; highest per-door cost; no retrofit onto existing hardware; heavy installation footprint |
| A5 | Do nothing / manual patrol + paper inspection logs [Status Quo] | — | Zero hardware cost; highest ongoing labor cost; detection lag measured in hours–shifts; compliance documentation gap at audits; zero P1 burden |

*"Do nothing" is the most common competitor in this market — most lessors delay until forced by audit findings or insurance requirements.*

---

### 2.5 Scenarios + Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Context | Active Output Types | Constraint overlay |
|---|---|---|---|
| SC1 — Portfolio-wide retrofit | REIT lessor deploying across 20+ buildings; mixed door ages and handle types | All | C3 (range in mixed construction), C6 (BMS protocol integration), C4 (battery at scale) |
| SC2 — Pre-occupancy fit-out | New tenant in Class B/C office; lessor installs exit monitoring before handover | Class B/C Office | C7 (price sensitivity of smaller asset) |
| SC3 — Medical office compliance | Fire marshal audit approaching; exits in patient-access areas need documentation | Medical Office | C8 (GDPR on patient-zone monitoring), C4 (battery in high-traffic ward exits) |
| SC4 — Smart-office BMS integration | Class A campus; tenant lease covenants include IoT monitoring requirements | Class A Office | C6 (BACnet/KNX interop required — primary friction point for Türwächter) |
| SC5 — Industrial flex retrofit | High daily exit use; steel frame attenuates RF; maintenance access infrequent | Industrial Flex | C4 (battery life under high activation frequency), C3 (RF range in steel), C9 (IP class for dusty environments) |

*Sparsity check: SC3 activates Medical Office only; SC4 activates Class A Office only; SC5 activates Industrial Flex only → Core Job passes sparsity test.*

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Burleson Pyramid — Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Who | Active Pyramid Levels |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | Facility Manager (FM) | P1-all, P2, P3, P4, P5 |
| **Job Overseer** | Asset Manager / Property Manager | P1-own, P2, P4 |
| **Job Beneficiary** | Building tenants (employees, visitors) | P2, P5 |
| **Lifecycle — Installer** | FM technician / FM contractor | P1-Preparation only |
| **Lifecycle — Maintainer** | FM technician (batteries, firmware, config) | P1-Maintenance only |
| **Purchase Executor** | CRE Director / CFO / Head of FM | P1-Acquisition only |
| **Purchase Influencer** | Fire safety inspector, insurance auditor, fire marshal | P2-own, P4 |

### P1 Lifecycle by Alternative (for Executor)

| Stage | A1 (Mechanical) | A2 (IoT — Türwächter) | A3 (Wired) | A5 (Do nothing) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acquire** | Specify per door type, procure, no qualification | Specify per door type + coverage topology, procure wireless receiver/gateway | Specify per door type, engage electrician, get installation quote | None |
| **Prepare** | Surface-mount on handle, battery in | Surface-mount, configure wireless channel, install receiver + gateway, test connectivity | Conduit + wire run per door, mount hardware, connect to FACP, commission | None |
| **Use** | Passive; alarm triggers on activation | Monitor dashboard; receive push alerts for events | Monitor via FACP panel; receive alarm panel notifications | Manual door patrol per shift |
| **Maintain** | Replace battery when unit fails (reactive); manual per-door check on inspection cycle | Monitor battery life dashboard; replace proactively; check signal status | Annual wired inspection; FACP firmware updates; no battery | Log paper inspection forms manually |
| **Dispose** | Remove unit from handle; no specialized disposal | Remove unit + receiver; no data erasure requirement (local device) | Decommission wired circuit; remove conduit if refurbishing | None |

*Key insight: A5 (Do nothing) has zero P1 burden but the highest ongoing labor cost and compliance risk — it is the dominant competitor because switching costs to any hardware solution are real.*

---

### 3.2 Job Map

| # | Step | Statement | Relevant? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DEFINE | Define which doors require emergency exit security, applicable standards (EN 179 / EN 1125), and portfolio integration requirements | No | Standard assessment activity; low error opportunity |
| 2 | LOCATE | Map all emergency exit door positions, wireless coverage topology, BMS infrastructure type | No | One-time survey; standard FM activity |
| 3 | PREPARE | Install door guardian units, configure wireless receivers and BUS gateways, program key overrides, integrate with monitoring platform | **YES** | Complex multi-door installation; high effort differential between A2 (wireless) and A3 (wired) |
| 4 | CONFIRM | Verify alarm triggering, wireless connectivity, battery status, compliance with EN 179 / EN 1125 | **YES** | High-stakes — false negatives have regulatory and safety consequences |
| 5 | EXECUTE | Continuously prevent unauthorized use while allowing single-motion evacuation; report events wirelessly | **YES** | Core value delivery; robustness and false-alarm rate are critical |
| 6 | MONITOR | Monitor door status, alarm events, battery health, and signal integrity across all units | **YES** | Primary IoT differentiation over A1; detection lag is the dominant pain point |
| 7 | MODIFY | Respond to alarms, replace batteries, reconfigure coverage, manage key-override access | **YES** | Recurring operational cost driver; battery management burden at portfolio scale |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Generate compliance documentation, audit logs, inspection reports for fire marshal and insurance reviews | **YES** | Direct value driver for commercial building lessor segment — compliance documentation gap is acute |

---

### 3.3 Market Needs (P1 + P2 Error Types)

**22 ErrorStatements** across 6 relevant job steps and 3 stakeholder roles.

#### Step 3 — PREPARE

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N01 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit security installation requiring permanent electrical infrastructure modifications to building fabric, measured as percentage of door deployments triggering conduit or wiring work | EFFORT | Executor / Lifecycle-Installer | PREPARE | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | false | 0.85 |
| N02 | Minimize the likelihood of wireless emergency exit monitoring commissioning requiring specialist RF survey support, measured as percentage of installations requiring dedicated RF coverage validation beyond standard FM technician scope | EFFORT | Lifecycle-Installer | PREPARE | 7 | 5 | 9 | time | **true** | 0.70 |
| N03 | Increase the ability to retrofit emergency exit security devices to existing commercial doors without structural modification, measured as percentage of existing EN 179 and EN 1125 door variants addressable without frame alteration | ROBUSTNESS | Executor | PREPARE | 8 | 5 | 11 | cost | false | 0.80 |

#### Step 4 — CONFIRM

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N04 | Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device failing to trigger an alarm upon unauthorized door activation, measured as false-negative alarm rate per 1,000 activations | ACCURACY | Executor | CONFIRM | 10 | 6 | 14 | safety | false | 0.90 |
| N05 | Increase the ability to verify that all emergency exit security devices across a portfolio are in functional alarm-ready state before a scheduled fire safety inspection, measured as confirmed-active device rate achievable without manual per-door physical testing | DETECTION | Executor / Overseer | CONFIRM | 9 | 3 | 15 | safety | false | 0.85 |
| N06 | Minimize the likelihood of incorrect wireless address assignment causing two door guardian units on adjacent doors to share a transmission channel, measured as address-collision incidents per 50-door deployment | ACCURACY | Lifecycle-Installer | CONFIRM | 7 | 5 | 9 | reliability | **true** | 0.65 |

#### Step 5 — EXECUTE

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N07 | Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device becoming non-functional during a building power interruption, measured as alarm activation availability rate under complete grid power loss | ROBUSTNESS | Executor | EXECUTE | 10 | 8 | 12 | safety | false | 0.95 |
| N08 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm devices triggering false positives from authorized key-override access by facility personnel, measured as false-alarm incidents per 100 authorized key activations per month | ACCURACY | Executor | EXECUTE | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | false | 0.80 |
| N09 | Minimize the likelihood of inconsistent alarm activation thresholds causing premature or late alarm triggers across door types with different handle configurations, measured as alarm force variance across lever-handle and panic-bar variants within the same building | CONSISTENCY | Executor / Beneficiary | EXECUTE | 7 | 6 | 8 | safety | false | 0.70 |

#### Step 6 — MONITOR

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N10 | Increase the ability to detect an emergency exit door being propped open or activated within 60 seconds of the event, measured as mean detection lag from door-open event to operator notification | DETECTION | Executor / Overseer | MONITOR | 9 | 2 | 16 | safety | false | 0.90 |
| N11 | Increase the ability to monitor real-time emergency exit door status across an entire commercial property portfolio from a single operator dashboard, measured as number of buildings and doors simultaneously visible per interface | THROUGHPUT | Overseer | MONITOR | 8 | 2 | 14 | time | false | 0.85 |
| N12 | Increase the ability to detect emergency exit monitoring device wireless connectivity failures before the next scheduled inspection cycle, measured as mean time from connectivity loss to operator notification | DETECTION | Executor | MONITOR | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | **true** | 0.75 |
| N13 | Increase the ability to detect approaching battery depletion in emergency exit monitoring devices before failure, measured as percentage of battery replacements performed before the device becomes non-functional | ROBUSTNESS | Lifecycle-Maintainer | MONITOR | 9 | 3 | 15 | reliability | **true** | 0.80 |

#### Step 7 — MODIFY

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N14 | Minimize the likelihood of battery replacement in emergency exit monitoring devices requiring specialist skills or triggering false alarm events, measured as percentage of battery replacements completable by standard building staff without alarm activation | EFFORT | Lifecycle-Maintainer | MODIFY | 7 | 5 | 9 | cost | **true** | 0.70 |
| N15 | Minimize the likelihood of on-site dispatch responses being required for non-emergency door activations that could be resolved remotely, measured as percentage of alarm events requiring physical visit vs. remotely resolvable via monitoring platform | EFFORT | Executor | MODIFY | 7 | 4 | 10 | cost | false | 0.75 |
| N16 | Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit monitoring losing BMS communication following network infrastructure updates, measured as connectivity restoration time after BMS or gateway firmware changes | ROBUSTNESS | Executor | MODIFY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | **true** | 0.70 |

#### Step 8 — CONCLUDE

| ID | Statement | Error Type | Role | Step | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related | Conf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N17 | Increase the ability to generate complete emergency exit compliance documentation meeting fire marshal requirements at any time without manual data entry, measured as documentation completeness score achievable at an unannounced inspection | DETECTION | Overseer / Influencer | CONCLUDE | 9 | 3 | 15 | safety | false | 0.85 |
| N18 | Minimize the likelihood of annual fire safety audit preparation requiring manual compilation of exit inspection records across multiple properties, measured as person-hours per property required to produce a complete compliance evidence package | EFFORT | Overseer | CONCLUDE | 8 | 3 | 13 | time | false | 0.85 |
| N19 | Minimize the likelihood of compliance documentation quality varying significantly between properties within a portfolio, measured as inter-property variance in documentation completeness scores at audit point | CONSISTENCY | Overseer | CONCLUDE | 7 | 3 | 11 | cost | false | 0.75 |

---

### 3.4 P3–P5 Needs (Bridge Questions)

| ID | Pyramid | Role | Retrieval | Bridge | Statement | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Prod-Related |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| N20 | P3 Role Identity | Executor (FM) | "By getting emergency exit compliance done well, the FM becomes a proactive safety professional who enables the asset manager without compliance surprises" | "What must the product do so they CAN maintain that professional standing?" | Increase the ability to provide asset managers with proactive emergency exit compliance status updates without manual report generation, measured as automated compliance status communications per property per week | 7 | 2 | 12 | time | false |
| N21 | P4 Image Identity | Overseer (Asset Mgr) watched by fire marshal, insurer, institutional investors | "Fire marshals and insurers must see a disciplined, documented compliance operator; investors must see low regulatory risk in the asset" | "What must the product do so those stakeholders SEE that impression?" | Increase the ability to produce portfolio-level emergency exit compliance evidence on demand during fire marshal inspections or insurance renewals, measured as time-to-produce a complete compliance evidence package from request to delivery | 8 | 3 | 13 | cost | false |
| N22 | P5 Emotional | Executor (FM) | "Avoid: the anxiety of discovering a non-compliant door for the first time during a fire marshal inspection" | "What must the product do so they DON'T feel that?" | Minimize the likelihood of a building operator discovering an emergency exit compliance failure for the first time during a scheduled fire marshal inspection rather than through proactive monitoring, measured as ratio of compliance failures detected pre-inspection vs. at inspection [hypothesis] | 8 | 2 | 14 | safety | false |

---

### 3.5 ODI Summary — Top Opportunities

| Rank | ID | Statement (abbreviated) | Opp | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | N10 | Detect door propped open within 60 seconds | **16** | Underserved |
| 2 | N05 | Verify all devices functional pre-inspection without physical testing | **15** | Underserved |
| 2 | N13 | Detect battery depletion before device failure | **15** | Underserved |
| 2 | N17 | Generate complete compliance docs without manual data entry | **15** | Underserved |
| 5 | N04 | Minimize false-negative alarm rate | **14** | Underserved |
| 5 | N11 | Monitor portfolio-wide exit status from single dashboard | **14** | Underserved |
| 5 | N22 | Avoid discovering compliance failure at inspection [hypothesis] | **14** | Underserved |
| 8 | N12 | Detect wireless connectivity failure before inspection | **13** | Underserved |
| 8 | N18 | Minimize manual compilation effort for audit | **13** | Underserved |
| 8 | N21 | Produce compliance evidence on demand | **13** | Underserved |

**Interpretation:** Top 4 underserved needs (N10, N05, N13, N17) are directly addressed by the Türwächter IoT variant and are UNMET by the dominant A1 alternative (mechanical-only). This is the primary wedge. The A3 alternative (wired electronic) addresses N10/N11/N17 but fails on N01/N03 (no-wiring retrofit constraint).

---

sections.title: Validation (QA Pass)
sections.content:
| # | Check | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| V1 | Format compliance | PASS | All 22 statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of..." or "Increase the ability to..." with one measurable KPI each |
| V2 | product_related classification | PASS | N02, N06, N12, N13, N14, N16 are technology-caused (disappear for A3 wired or A1 mechanical); all others are market-inherent |
| V3 | Importance/satisfaction sanity | PASS | Safety/regulatory needs (N04, N07) scored 10; no uniform clustering; A5 satisfaction scores 1–2 where appropriate |
| V4 | Impact category accuracy | PASS | safety for alarm reliability; cost for labor/install; time for detection/monitoring lag; reliability for connectivity |
| V5 | Coverage | PASS | 6 relevant steps × ≥3 needs each + 3 P3–P5; 73% market-inherent / 27% technology-specific; 22 total needs |
| V6 | Solution-agnostic | PASS | Zero references to "Türwächter", "GfS", "EasyWave", "868 MHz", "piezo", or specific mechanism in need statements |
| V7 | Anchor correctness | PASS | L4 anchor (Door Guardian Alarm Unit) — component-level, matches product abstraction |
| V8 | Product job references parent | PASS | "Help Emergency Exit Security to prevent unauthorized use..." — parent L5 unit named explicitly |

---

sections.title: Cross-Component Notes
sections.content:
**Constraint overlay:** C3 (RF range) surfaces in N02 and N12. C4 (battery life) drives N13 and N14. C6 (EasyWave lock-in) drives N16. C8 (GDPR) not surfaced in needs (addressed in Constraints section); applicable to SC3 deployment context.

**Highest differentiation gap vs. A1 (dominant incumbent):** N10, N11, N13, N17, N18 — the entire IoT monitoring and compliance documentation cluster. This is where the Türwächter IoT variant wins.

**Segment priority:**
- S1 (REIT/portfolio): Needs N11, N18, N19, N21 are highest-value for this segment.
- S3 (Medical office): Needs N04, N05, N17, N22 are highest-value (regulatory stakes are highest).
- S5 (Smart-office): Needs N16 (BMS integration robustness) is the primary barrier; C6 constraint must be mitigated.

---

---

*Sources for factual claims:*
- *Annual fire door inspection requirement: [NFPA 80 — Inspect Point](https://www.inspectpoint.com/nfpa-emergency-exit-door-requirements/) | Germany: ASR A2.3 / Bundesland building codes*
- *Fire door certification cost $200–800/door: [Commercial Door Compliance — Advance Door](https://www.advance-door.com/2025/05/19/ensuring-your-commercial-doors-are-code-compliant/)*
- *IoT monitoring operational cost reduction 15–30%: [IoT Property Monitoring — Aeologic](https://www.aeologic.com/blog/how-iot-cloud-are-redefining-property-monitoring-automation/)*
- *EN 179 / EN 1125 standards: [GEZE Emergency Exit Protection](https://www.geze.com/en/discover/topics/emergency-exit-protection) | [Intertek EN 179](https://www.intertek.com/building/standards/en-179/)*
- *Multi-site compliance solutions: [Everon Commercial Buildings](https://www.everonsolutions.com/industries/commercial-buildings)*

rows.CFJ (extracted from description): Provide leasable nonresidential building space to tenant organizations while maintaining regulatory-compliant safety infrastructure and preserving long-term asset value
rows.Output Types: Class A Office, Class B/C Office, Retail Commercial, Industrial Flex, Medical Office
rows.Offering Level: L7 (Ecosystem) — lessor coordinates owners, tenants, FM providers, regulators; does not produce a physical product

rows.Defining Circumstance: Dozens–hundreds of properties; centralized FM team; compliance tracked at portfolio level
rows.Türwächter Relevance: HIGH — portfolio dashboard and centralized compliance reporting are the primary value lever

rows.Defining Circumstance: 1–5 buildings; FM outsourced; decisions made per-building
rows.Türwächter Relevance: MEDIUM — value argument more price-driven; IoT premium harder to justify

rows.Defining Circumstance: Patient-access corridors; HIPAA overlay; fire marshal audits more frequent
rows.Türwächter Relevance: HIGH — regulatory pressure intensity is highest; battery-powered wireless is preferred over mains for retrofit

rows.Defining Circumstance: High pedestrian throughput; guest-facing stairwell exits; frequent misuse
rows.Türwächter Relevance: HIGH — frequent alarm events; detection lag most painful

rows.Defining Circumstance: BMS-native tenants; KNX/BACnet infrastructure; tenants demand IoT-integrated systems
rows.Türwächter Relevance: HIGH (with friction) — IoT value clear but EasyWave protocol lock-in (C6) creates BMS integration barrier

rows.Defining Circumstance: High daily exit-door traffic; infrequent maintenance visits; steel-frame buildings
rows.Türwächter Relevance: MEDIUM — battery life (C4) and RF attenuation (C3) elevate constraint exposure

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN (product serves Building Operations; never becomes part of the tenant's space)

Türwächter is a component (L4) within the Emergency Exit Security system (L5). It requires Wireless Monitoring Receiver + BUS Gateway to form the complete L5 solution. Does not anchor at L5 — it is not the full system.
Parent unit (Emergency Exit Security, L5) defines the job: everything the product must do derives from what Emergency Exit Security needs within L6d Building Operations.
Secure (from Emergency Exit Security L5 job_family: "secure emergency exits against unauthorized use while maintaining unimpeded evacuation capability")
Building Operations team — specifically the Facility Manager
"What securing does a Building Operations team need to achieve for emergency exits across a multi-tenant commercial property portfolio?"
Building Operations (L6d) activates for ALL output types — every building type has mandated emergency exits
Wireless IoT variant best fits: S1 (portfolio), S3 (medical office), S5 (smart-office with BMS integration budget). Less suited: S2 (price-sensitive), S6 (RF constraints).

rows.Alternative: Mechanical door alarm guards, no connectivity (~40% share)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: No remote monitoring; compliance issues discovered during physical inspection (hours–days lag); no battery monitoring; lowest per-door cost

rows.Alternative: **Wireless IoT exit alarm guards ← Türwächter** (~20% share)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Battery dependency; EasyWave protocol not natively interoperable with BACnet/KNX (C6); 30 m RF range limit (C3); higher per-door cost (~730 EUR, C7)

rows.Alternative: Wired electronic exit control, alarm-panel-integrated (~25% share)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Requires mains wiring + conduit to each door; electrician for install and maintenance; not retrofit-friendly in occupied buildings; full FACP integration possible

rows.Alternative: Integrated alarmed panic hardware (e-Bar type) (~15% share)
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Replaces entire panic bar assembly; highest per-door cost; no retrofit onto existing hardware; heavy installation footprint

rows.Alternative: Do nothing / manual patrol + paper inspection logs [Status Quo]
rows.Inherent Trade-offs: Zero hardware cost; highest ongoing labor cost; detection lag measured in hours–shifts; compliance documentation gap at audits; zero P1 burden

rows.Context: REIT lessor deploying across 20+ buildings; mixed door ages and handle types
rows.Constraint overlay: C3 (range in mixed construction), C6 (BMS protocol integration), C4 (battery at scale)

rows.Context: New tenant in Class B/C office; lessor installs exit monitoring before handover
Fire marshal audit approaching; exits in patient-access areas need documentation
rows.Constraint overlay: C8 (GDPR on patient-zone monitoring), C4 (battery in high-traffic ward exits)

rows.Context: Class A campus; tenant lease covenants include IoT monitoring requirements
rows.Constraint overlay: C6 (BACnet/KNX interop required — primary friction point for Türwächter)

rows.Context: High daily exit use; steel frame attenuates RF; maintenance access infrequent
rows.Constraint overlay: C4 (battery life under high activation frequency), C3 (RF range in steel), C9 (IP class for dusty environments)

rows.Who: CRE Director / CFO / Head of FM
Fire safety inspector, insurance auditor, fire marshal

rows.A1 (Mechanical): Specify per door type, procure, no qualification
rows.A2 (IoT — Türwächter): Specify per door type + coverage topology, procure wireless receiver/gateway
rows.A3 (Wired): Specify per door type, engage electrician, get installation quote

rows.A2 (IoT — Türwächter): Surface-mount, configure wireless channel, install receiver + gateway, test connectivity
rows.A3 (Wired): Conduit + wire run per door, mount hardware, connect to FACP, commission

rows.A2 (IoT — Türwächter): Monitor dashboard; receive push alerts for events
rows.A3 (Wired): Monitor via FACP panel; receive alarm panel notifications

rows.A1 (Mechanical): Replace battery when unit fails (reactive); manual per-door check on inspection cycle
rows.A2 (IoT — Türwächter): Monitor battery life dashboard; replace proactively; check signal status
rows.A3 (Wired): Annual wired inspection; FACP firmware updates; no battery

rows.A1 (Mechanical): Remove unit from handle; no specialized disposal
rows.A2 (IoT — Türwächter): Remove unit + receiver; no data erasure requirement (local device)
rows.A3 (Wired): Decommission wired circuit; remove conduit if refurbishing

entities.product_job_statement: Help Emergency Exit Security to prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors across a multi-tenant commercial property while maintaining unimpeded evacuation compliance, providing portfolio-level status visibility, and generating audit-ready compliance records — without requiring mains wiring at each door point
entities.cfj_statement: Provide leasable nonresidential building space to tenant organizations while maintaining regulatory-compliant safety infrastructure and preserving long-term asset value

Install door guardian units, configure wireless receivers and BUS gateways, program key overrides, and integrate with monitoring platform

entities.statement: Verify alarm triggering, wireless connectivity, battery status, and compliance with EN 179 / EN 1125 across all installed units
entities.relevance_rationale: High-stakes verification; false negatives have direct regulatory and safety consequences

entities.relevance_rationale: Core value delivery; alarm reliability and false-positive rate are critical quality dimensions

entities.relevance_rationale: Primary IoT differentiation point over mechanical alternatives; detection lag is the dominant pain point

entities.rationale: 40% incumbent (mechanical, A1) requires no wiring; 25% wired (A3) requires electrician and conduit per door — averaged satisfaction 3-4 across alternatives

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of wireless emergency exit monitoring commissioning requiring specialist RF survey support, measured as percentage of installations requiring dedicated RF coverage validation beyond standard FM technician scope
entities.rationale: Wireless IoT category only; wired and mechanical alternatives do not have this commissioning step

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to retrofit emergency exit security devices to existing commercial doors without structural modification, measured as percentage of existing EN 179 and EN 1125 door variants addressable without frame alteration
entities.rationale: Most commercial buildings are existing stock; retrofit capability is a baseline market requirement regardless of technology choice

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device failing to trigger an alarm upon unauthorized door activation, measured as false-negative alarm rate per 1,000 activations
entities.rationale: Regulatory and life-safety imperative; all alternatives must address; mechanical springs rated well but can wear; wired electronic well-addressed; mechanical-no-connectivity partially addressed

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to verify that all emergency exit security devices across a portfolio are in functional alarm-ready state before a scheduled fire safety inspection, measured as confirmed-active device rate achievable without manual per-door physical testing
entities.rationale: Dominant incumbent (A1 mechanical) requires physical per-door testing; IoT variants (A2) enable remote status check; manual verification for 50+ doors is prohibitively time-intensive

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect wireless address assignment causing two door guardian units on adjacent doors to share a transmission channel, measured as address-collision incidents per 50-door deployment
entities.rationale: Wireless IoT category only; address collision is a commissioning risk unique to multi-unit wireless deployments

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit security device becoming non-functional during a building power interruption, measured as alarm activation availability rate under complete grid power loss
entities.rationale: Regulatory requirement per EN 179/1125 and EltVTR; mechanical-primary design satisfies this for A1, A2, A4; wired electronic (A3) must use UPS — well addressed across market

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit alarm devices triggering false positives from authorized key-override access by facility personnel, measured as false-alarm incidents per 100 authorized key activations per month
entities.rationale: High-frequency pain in multi-tenant buildings; authorized maintenance access and delivery routines create frequent false alarms; key override partially addresses this but requires training

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of inconsistent alarm activation thresholds causing premature or late alarm triggers across door types with different handle configurations, measured as alarm force variance across lever-handle and panic-bar variants within the same building
entities.rationale: Mixed handle configurations (EN 179 lever + EN 1125 panic bar) common in large commercial buildings; force calibration during installation determines consistency

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to detect an emergency exit door being propped open or activated within 60 seconds of the event, measured as mean detection lag from door-open event to operator notification
entities.rationale: A1 (mechanical-only): detection lag = hours to days (next patrol or inspection). A2 (IoT): near-real-time detection. A5 (do nothing): detection lag = shift or longer. Dominant competitor A1 scores satisfaction 1–2 on this need.

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to monitor real-time emergency exit door status across an entire commercial property portfolio from a single operator dashboard, measured as number of buildings and doors simultaneously visible per interface
entities.rationale: No existing solution addresses portfolio-level exit monitoring for commercial lessors at scale; A1/A5 have zero dashboard capability; A3 limited to per-building FACP

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to detect emergency exit monitoring device wireless connectivity failures before the next scheduled inspection cycle, measured as mean time from connectivity loss to operator notification
entities.rationale: Wireless IoT category only; wired electronic (A3) and mechanical (A1) do not have wireless connectivity to lose. A silent disconnection creates an unmonitored door — regulatory gap equivalent to a failed unit.

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to detect approaching battery depletion in emergency exit monitoring devices before failure, measured as percentage of battery replacements performed before the device becomes non-functional
entities.rationale: Battery-powered category only; mechanical (A1) and wired (A3) do not have this risk. A portfolio of 100+ battery units creates a significant unplanned maintenance burden without proactive battery monitoring.

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of battery replacement in emergency exit monitoring devices requiring specialist skills or triggering false alarm events, measured as percentage of battery replacements completable by standard building staff without alarm activation
entities.rationale: Battery replacement procedure specific to battery-powered IoT category; design of the battery access mechanism determines whether a false alarm is triggered during servicing

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of on-site dispatch responses being required for non-emergency door activations that could be resolved remotely, measured as percentage of alarm events requiring physical site visit vs. remotely resolvable via monitoring platform
entities.rationale: Relevant across all alternatives; IoT monitoring enables remote triage to distinguish genuine incidents from authorized access without dispatch

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit monitoring losing BMS communication following network infrastructure updates, measured as connectivity restoration time after BMS or gateway firmware changes
entities.rationale: Wireless IoT integration category; EasyWave protocol lock-in (C6) makes BMS re-integration after updates non-trivial; wired systems on open protocols (BACnet) handle this more gracefully

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to generate complete emergency exit compliance documentation meeting fire marshal requirements at any time without manual data entry, measured as documentation completeness score achievable at an unannounced inspection
entities.rationale: Annual fire safety inspection required in most EU/US jurisdictions (NFPA 80: annual; Germany: ASR A2.3 / Bundesland-specific). Dominant A1 incumbent generates no logs; A5 requires manual spreadsheet; IoT enables automated timestamped records.

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of annual fire safety audit preparation requiring manual compilation of exit inspection records across multiple properties, measured as person-hours per property required to produce a complete compliance evidence package
entities.rationale: Portfolio-scale compliance preparation is a significant annual cost center for institutional lessors; estimated 4–8 person-hours per property per audit cycle with manual methods (certification + inspection costs $200–800 per door per US data)

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of compliance documentation quality varying significantly between properties within a portfolio, measured as inter-property variance in documentation completeness scores at audit point
entities.rationale: Multi-property lessors rely on mixed-quality FM contractors across the portfolio; inconsistent documentation creates audit exposure in the weakest properties

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to provide asset managers with proactive emergency exit compliance status updates without manual report generation, measured as automated compliance status communications per property per week
entities.rationale: FM's professional identity requires demonstrating proactive safety management upward to asset managers; today this requires manual reporting and personal follow-up

entities.full_statement: Increase the ability to produce portfolio-level emergency exit compliance evidence on demand during fire marshal inspections or insurance renewals, measured as time-to-produce a complete compliance evidence package from request to delivery
entities.rationale: Insurance underwriters and institutional investors (REITs) scrutinize fire safety compliance records; on-demand audit packages are a tangible signal of operational discipline

entities.full_statement: Minimize the likelihood of a building operator discovering an emergency exit compliance failure for the first time during a scheduled fire marshal inspection rather than through proactive monitoring, measured as ratio of compliance failures detected pre-inspection vs. at inspection point [hypothesis]
entities.rationale: [hypothesis — requires validation through FM interviews] The anxiety of being caught off-guard at an inspection is a strong latent motivator; currently a1/A5 operators have no mechanism for pre-inspection discovery
Dominant incumbent; lowest cost; zero monitoring capability; all compliance documentation manual
Türwächter's category; growing but constrained by price premium and BMS integration friction
Best monitoring capability but highest install cost and retrofit barrier; preferred in new construction and high-security contexts
Replaces panic bar assembly; highest cost; no retrofit capability; used primarily in new-build high-traffic public buildings
